title: JD Vance's crusade against GDP is wrong and bad
gist: Smith argues the American New Right's attack on GDP as a metric is a rhetorical cover for trade and immigration restrictions that will make Americans poorer, borrowed from European degrowth discourse, and that historically it was rapid GDP growth (not deprivation) that produced the community, family, and fertility the right idealizes.

insights.claim: The American right's attacks on GDP as a metric are a rhetorical device to neutralize the 'this will make America poorer' objection to trade and immigration restrictions, borrowed and re-skinned from the European left's degrowth arguments.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Frame anti-GDP political rhetoric as a signal that materially costly protectionist/immigration policy is coming, not as a genuine measurement critique.
insights.quote: So America's right borrowed an argument from the European left. The European left favors degrowth, and another term for degrowth is “making GDP go down on purpose”.

insights.claim: JD Vance has emerged as the consensus leader of the New Right and is codifying anti-GDP, anti-economics arguments in a new book, signaling degrowth-adjacent framing entering mainstream right-wing policy.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Watch Vance's rhetoric as a leading indicator of Republican economic policy direction toward accepting deliberate material sacrifice.
insights.locator: JD Vance, who has emerged as the consensus leader

themes.theme: Growth caused the 1950s idyll, not deprivation

insights.claim: The mid-century American peak of church attendance, fertility, and social solidarity coincided with the most rapid GDP-per-capita growth in US history, which nearly tripled over the period, implying growth enabled rather than eroded community.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Undercuts the thesis that reversing material abundance restores social cohesion; growth and social capital have historically moved together.
insights.quote: Over the period in which our social solidarity was soaring, our GDP per capita nearly tripled
insights.locator: It's important to remember that this all came

insights.claim: The Baby Boom was substantially driven by economic growth lowering the cost of children: US maternal death rates fell 94% between 1936 and 1956 (from 51 to under 3 per 10,000 live births), while labor-saving appliances and a postwar housing boom cut the cost of raising families.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Pro-natalist policy that reduces incomes contradicts the historical mechanism; falling cost-of-children (housing, appliances, medical safety) is what raised fertility.
insights.quote: Between 1936 and 1956, America's maternal death rate fell by 94 percent, from 51 deaths per 10,000 live births to under 3
insights.locator: Parenthood rapidly became much easier and safer

insights.claim: Fertility rises above replacement mainly below roughly $15,000 GDP per person versus over $90,000 in the US today, and even that relationship is weakening as Sub-Saharan African fertility plunges, so no realistic amount of deliberate impoverishment would restore high US fertility.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Pro-natalist bets premised on degrowth are quantitatively hopeless; the fertility-poverty link is both extreme and eroding globally.
insights.locator: Now it's worth noting that if you were to decrease

insights.claim: The opioid epidemic reduced US GDP rather than raising it, costing roughly $700 billion (about 3.4% of GDP) in 2018 alone and over $2.5 trillion from 2015 to 2018 per CEA analysis cited by the Philadelphia Fed.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Curbing opioid abuse is GDP-accretive; anti-GDP rhetoric that treats addiction spending as growth misreads the sign of the effect.
insights.quote: the annual (nominal) economic cost of the opioid epidemic, including the cost of lives lost, is estimated at about $700 billion (roughly 3.4 percent of GDP) in 2018 alone

insights.claim: Vance conflates correlation with causation by treating creature comforts as the cause of spiritual malaise without explaining any mechanism by which removing houses, SUVs, or air conditioning would produce closer families or stronger communities.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: The core logical gap in New Right anti-consumerism means its policy prescriptions lack a causal theory of how they achieve their social goals.
insights.locator: But Vance's real problem is that he conflates

themes.theme: Why tariffs and deportation won't deliver

insights.claim: US manufacturing jobs have fallen since Trump took office in 2025, and tariffs are hurting the manufacturing sector by raising the cost of intermediate goods, contradicting the premise that protectionism restores good manufacturing jobs.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Positioning against a tariff-driven manufacturing renaissance; input-cost tariffs are a net drag on domestic industrial employment.
insights.quote: We've lost manufacturing jobs since Trump took office last year!
insights.locator: On trade, you've seen Trump explicitly make the argument

insights.claim: Putnam's diversity-lowers-trust finding is tiny in magnitude: going from zero to maximum diversity reduces social trust by only about 1 point on a 100-point scale, per Bryan Caplan, implying mass deportations would barely move social cohesion.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: The empirical case that immigration restriction restores community is quantitatively negligible even taking the diversity-trust research at face value.
insights.quote: going from zero diversity to maximum diversity reduces social trust by the equivalent of 1 point on a 100-point scale

insights.claim: Okun's Law establishes a strong negative correlation between unemployment and quarterly GDP growth, and the strongest working-class wage gains since the 1960s occurred in 2014-2024 because steady growth held the economy near full employment.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Anti-growth policy directly threatens the full-employment conditions that produce blue-collar wage growth via aggregate demand.
insights.quote: The greatest period of growth for working-class wages in America since the 1960s was 2014-2024, despite inflation and the pandemic, because steady GDP growth kept us at full employment
insights.locator: And only when GDP is growing robustly

insights.claim: Selective high-skilled immigration raises per-capita GDP through composition effects, market-size returns to scale, and boosted innovation, whereas low-skilled immigration mainly serves total-GDP/military-scale and distributional (eldercare) goals.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Distinguish per-capita versus total-GDP objectives when reading immigration policy; skill-selectivity is the lever for per-capita wealth.

themes.theme: The New Right has built nothing

insights.claim: The New Right has created no new community organizations, institutions, or religious revival to replace what it critiques, making its anti-modernity stance destructive without a constructive program.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Judge the movement by its lack of institution-building; its agenda is subtractive, which limits any durable social payoff even on its own terms.
insights.quote: So far, the New Right has built nothing — no new community organizations or institutions, no religious revival, nothing that would knit our society together.
insights.locator: Look, I also want America to have a stronger society
